content.text: Scorecard Review Cadence and Action Planning

Generating a health scorecard without a defined review cadence produces dashboards that nobody reads. The cadence determines who reviews which metrics, how often, in what forum, and — critically — what actions result from the review. Without this structure, scorecard metrics become decorative: teams are vaguely aware that a metric exists but have no shared understanding of when a number is bad enough to act on.

This sub-subtopic defines four review rhythms — daily, weekly, monthly, and quarterly — with distinct purposes, owners, time budgets, and action triggers for each. It also describes the action planning process that converts scorecard findings into trackable work.

attrs.alt: Scorecard Review Cadence — Four Rhythm Levels

content.text: Daily — On-Call Dashboard Review

The daily review is not a meeting. It is the on-call engineer's five-minute morning check of the live scorecard dashboard. Its purpose is to catch threshold crossings overnight and ensure any Red metrics are already tracked before the team's working day begins.
The on-call engineer checks three things:
Red metrics.
Any metric in Red status that was not Red at the previous handoff must have a tracking ticket. If a ticket does not exist, create one before doing anything else.
Amber trends.
An Amber metric that has been Amber for more than five consecutive days without an associated backlog item is a process failure. Create the backlog item and assign it.
Overnight anomalies.

Check the cost anomaly alert log and the error rate chart for the past 12 hours. A spike that resolved before business hours but has no explanation in the incident log is worth a brief investigation note.

The daily review produces at most two outputs: a ticket if a new Red metric appeared, and a brief note in the on-call handoff document ("eval pass rate dropped to 91% overnight — ticket #4821 created"). It is intentionally lightweight so it is done consistently rather than skipped when the team is busy.
Weekly — Team Lead Digest and Review

The weekly review is a 20-minute synchronous meeting, typically held on Monday morning, anchored by the automatically generated weekly digest. The digest is posted to the team's Slack channel by a scheduled CI job at 08:00 on Monday morning, giving team members time to read it before the meeting.
Weekly Digest Format
The digest follows a fixed template to ensure comparability across weeks:

## GenAI Service Weekly Scorecard — Week of 2025-07-01

### Summary
- Overall status: 🟡 AMBER (1 amber, 0 red)
- Week-over-week trend: Stable

### Metrics

| Dimension         | Metric                  | Current | Last Week | Status |
|-------------------|-------------------------|---------|-----------|--------|
| Quality           | Eval pass rate          | 93.2%   | 94.1%     | 🟡 AMBER |
| Quality           | Hallucination rate      | 1.4%    | 1.6%      | 🟢 GREEN |
| Cost              | Cost per request        | $0.0042 | $0.0041   | 🟢 GREEN |
| Cost              | Cache hit rate          | 45%     | 43%       | 🟢 GREEN |
| Reliability       | Availability            | 99.7%   | 99.8%     | 🟢 GREEN |
| Reliability       | p99 latency             | 2.1s    | 1.9s      | 🟢 GREEN |
| Security          | Open HIGH CVEs          | 0       | 0         | 🟢 GREEN |
| Delivery          | Deploy frequency        | 4/week  | 5/week    | 🟢 GREEN |
| Observability     | Trace coverage          | 100%    | 100%      | 🟢 GREEN |

### Amber Metric: Eval pass rate (93.2%)
- Cause: 3 new adversarial test cases added on 2025-06-28; 2 are currently failing
- Owner: @alice
- Action: Fix prompt boundary conditions by 2025-07-04
- Ticket: #4821

### Actions Due This Week
- #4821: Fix eval failures for adversarial boundary cases (due 2025-07-04, @alice)
- #4798: Upgrade langchain-core from 0.3.15 to 0.3.22 (due 2025-07-05, @bob)

### Prior Actions Completed Last Week
- #4776: Add eval cases for multi-turn memory feature ✅
- #4789: Reduce system prompt from 3200 to 2100 tokens (CPR improvement) ✅

The meeting reviews only the Amber and Red rows and the actions table. Green metrics are not discussed unless a team member flags a trend that is approaching a threshold. Time-boxing to 20 minutes enforces this discipline.
Monthly — Engineering Health Review

The monthly review is a 45-minute synchronous meeting attended by the team lead and engineering manager. Its purpose is pattern recognition: finding issues that do not appear in a single week's scorecard but become visible over a four-week view.
Monthly Review Agenda
Time
Agenda Item
Output
0–5 min
Month-over-month scorecard comparison (current vs 4 weeks ago)
Identify improving, stable, and degrading metrics
5–15 min
Persistent Amber metrics (Amber for ≥ 2 weeks)
Escalate to action plan or accept as new baseline
Post-incident review follow-up tracking
Confirm PIR action items are in the backlog with owners
Dependency audit summary and upgrade debt report
Assign upgrade work for the coming month
Budget forecast vs actuals
Adjust spend projections; identify cost efficiency opportunities
Actions for next month
List of 3–5 specific actions with owners and due dates

The most valuable part of the monthly review is the persistent Amber discussion. A metric that has been Amber for two consecutive weeks with the same action item assigned means the action item is not being completed. The monthly review is the forum where the engineering manager decides whether to re-prioritise the work, accept a higher threshold, or escalate.

A persistent Amber metric is not a reporting problem — it is a prioritisation problem. The engineering manager's job in the monthly review is to make a decision: fix it this month or accept it as the new Green. Leaving it Amber indefinitely is not a decision; it is drift.
Quarterly — Strategy and Threshold Reset

The quarterly review is a 90-minute session attended by engineering leads and the engineering director. It takes a strategic view of the scorecard: are the right things being measured? Are the thresholds still appropriate? Are there systematic issues across the service portfolio that require platform-level solutions?
Threshold Review Process
At each quarterly review, every metric threshold is evaluated against the team's actual performance over the trailing three months:
Metrics consistently Green.

If a metric has been Green for 10 of the past 13 weeks, the threshold is not challenging enough. Tighten by 10–20% to keep the metric as a meaningful signal. Example: eval pass rate target raised from 95% to 97%.
Metrics consistently Amber.

If a metric has been Amber for more than 6 of the past 13 weeks, either the target is unrealistic or the team lacks the capacity to improve. Investigate the root cause. If the target is realistic but deprioritised, escalate. If the target is genuinely too aggressive for the current service maturity, adjust the Amber threshold (not the Green target) to reflect a realistic improvement trajectory.
Metrics newly introduced.

Any new capability added in the quarter should have at least one metric added to the scorecard. New metrics start without a threshold for one quarter — the first quarter of data establishes the baseline, and thresholds are set at the next quarterly review.
Retired metrics.
Metrics for deprecated features or capabilities that are no longer relevant are removed from the scorecard. Announce the retirement in the weekly digest two weeks before removing to avoid confusion.
Cross-Service Benchmarking at the Quarterly Review
The platform team presents a cross-service benchmarking report at each quarterly review. Services are compared anonymously (or with consent, by name) on shared metrics to identify systemic patterns:
Finding Type
Implication
Platform Action
All services have eval coverage < 70%
Systemic process gap — DoD is not being enforced
Platform team runs eval coverage workshop with all teams
One service has 10x higher CPR than peers
Service-specific prompt inefficiency
Platform team schedules prompt review session with that team
Three services had the same CVE active for > 30 days
Dependency upgrade process is too slow
Platform team reviews the upgrade cadence policy and CI gates
All services improved reliability in Q3
Infrastructure investment paying off
Document and share the playbook across other services
Action Planning — Converting Findings to Work

A scorecard review without an action planning process produces lists of observations with no follow-through. The action planning process has three components: ticket creation, sprint integration, and completion tracking.
Ticket Creation Standards
Every Amber or Red scorecard finding that is not resolved within the same day requires a tracking ticket. The ticket must contain:
Metric and current value.
"Eval pass rate: 91% (threshold: 95%)"
Root cause hypothesis.
"Two new adversarial test cases added on 2025-06-28 are currently failing due to boundary condition in the safety classifier."
Proposed action.
"Fix the boundary condition in the classifier; update the prompt to handle edge cases."
Owner.
A single named engineer, not a team.
Due date.
Amber: within one sprint (≤ 2 weeks). Red: within 3 business days.
Scorecard link.
Link to the specific metric in the dashboard so reviewers can verify resolution.
Sprint Integration

Scorecard action items are not optional work. They must be included in sprint planning as first-class backlog items, not added as "if we have time" tasks. The team lead assigns a capacity budget for scorecard actions at the start of each sprint — typically 10–15% of total capacity, scaled up if Red metrics are present.
The sprint planning template includes a "Scorecard Health" section at the top:

## Sprint Planning — 2025-07-07

### Scorecard Health (review before feature work)
- Open Red metrics: 0
- Open Amber metrics: 1 (eval pass rate — ticket #4821)
- Scorecard action capacity: 2 story points (15% of 13sp sprint)
- Scorecard items in this sprint: #4821 (2sp, @alice, due 2025-07-11)

### Feature Work
[...]
Completion Tracking

When a scorecard action item is completed, the ticket is closed with a "resolution" note that explains what was done and links to the PR. The next weekly digest automatically reflects the improved metric. The team lead confirms in the weekly meeting that the metric returned to Green — visual confirmation that the action produced the expected result.

Incomplete action items that carry over from one sprint to the next receive a priority escalation: a backlog item that was not completed in its first sprint is moved to the top of the next sprint's scorecard section, regardless of other priorities. This prevents scorecard debt from accumulating silently.
Automating the Weekly Digest

The weekly digest is the keystone of the review cadence — it lands in everyone's inbox before the Monday meeting and sets the agenda. Automating it eliminates the risk of the digest being skipped when the team lead is on leave or the week is unusually busy.
The digest script reads from two JSON files:
scorecard-latest.json
(the current week's computed scorecard) and
scorecard-previous.json

(last week's, stored after each run for comparison). The script posts to Slack and optionally sends an email to engineering leadership. Replace the Slack webhook URL with the real one from secrets management.
Escalation Paths for Unresolved Metrics

The review cadence describes what happens when metrics are reviewed. The escalation path describes what happens when metrics are reviewed but not resolved — when action items are created but not completed, or when a metric resists improvement despite effort.
Situation
Escalation
Escalation Owner
Amber metric for 2+ weeks with no backlog item
Engineering manager creates the item and assigns it in the next sprint
Engineering manager
Amber metric for 4+ weeks with backlog item not completed
Engineering manager discusses deprioritisation with team lead; if deprioritised, accept new threshold or reassign to platform team
Engineering manager
Red metric open for 48+ hours
Engineering director notified; P1 incident opened if not already active
Engineering director
Same metric Red in two consecutive months
Platform team conducts root cause investigation; architectural change may be required
Platform team
Action item overdue by 1 week
Engineering manager escalates to team lead; item becomes top priority in next sprint
Engineering manager
Quarterly threshold reset declined by team
Engineering director arbitrates; thresholds are not optional negotiations
Engineering director

Escalation paths must be documented in the team's runbook and known to all engineers. An escalation path that exists only in the engineering manager's head is not an escalation path — it is an expectation that will not be met consistently.
Tracking Improvement Trends

Single-point scorecard values tell you the current state. Trend data tells you whether the team is improving. Store the weekly scorecard values in a time-series database (or even a simple JSON log file) and compute trend direction for each metric:

# scripts/scorecard_trend.py
import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import date, timedelta

def compute_trend(metric_history: list[float], window: int = 4) -> str:
    """
    Compare the average of the last 'window' weeks against the prior 'window' weeks.
    Returns: 'improving', 'stable', or 'degrading'
    """
    if len(metric_history) < window * 2:
        return "insufficient_data"

    recent = metric_history[-window:]
    prior = metric_history[-window * 2:-window]

    recent_avg = sum(recent) / len(recent)
    prior_avg = sum(prior) / len(prior)

    # For metrics where higher is better (pass rate, availability, cache hit rate)
    delta = recent_avg - prior_avg
    if abs(delta) < 0.005:  # Less than 0.5% change = stable
        return "stable"
    return "improving" if delta > 0 else "degrading"


def generate_trend_report(history_file: str) -> dict:
    history = json.loads(Path(history_file).read_text())
    report = {}

    for dimension, metrics in history.items():
        report[dimension] = {}
        for metric_name, values in metrics.items():
            report[dimension][metric_name] = {
                "current": values[-1],
                "4w_ago": values[-4] if len(values) >= 4 else None,
                "trend": compute_trend(values),
            }

    return report


if __name__ == "__main__":
    report = generate_trend_report("scorecard-history.json")
    print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))

The trend report surfaces three states: improving, stable, and degrading. Include the trend direction alongside the RAG status in the weekly digest. A Green metric that is degrading is more urgent than an Amber metric that is improving — the trend direction predicts where each metric is heading, giving the team time to act before a threshold is crossed.
Meeting Hygiene for Review Cadences

Review meetings are the most common failure point for scorecard programmes. They either become too long (teams discuss every Green metric at length) or stop happening (team skips them when busy). Four practices keep reviews effective:
Pre-read required, meeting optional for Green runs.
When every metric is Green and no action items are due, the weekly review can be replaced by a Slack acknowledgement from each team member. Reserve meeting time for Amber and Red situations.
Dashboard-first, not slides-first.
All reviews start with the live dashboard, not a prepared slide deck. Slide decks introduce selection bias and lag. The dashboard shows reality; slides show someone's interpretation of reality.
Time-box strictly.
A daily review that runs over 10 minutes is not a daily review — it is an ad-hoc incident response that happened to start with the dashboard. Set a timer and exit when it fires.
Actions captured during the meeting.
Action items are written into tickets during the meeting, not after. The meeting ends when the tickets are filed, not when the last slide is shown.
Summary

Scorecard reviews operate at four cadences: daily (on-call async check for Red metrics, 5 minutes), weekly (team lead digest review with action item triage, 20 minutes), monthly (engineering manager pattern review with budget and PIR tracking, 45 minutes), and quarterly (director-level strategy review with threshold resets and cross-service benchmarking, 90 minutes). Each cadence has a defined owner, time budget, and output. Scorecard findings convert to action items via a ticket creation standard with named owners and due dates; action items enter sprint planning as first-class work items with a reserved capacity budget. Meeting hygiene — pre-reads, dashboard-first, strict time boxes, tickets filed during the meeting — keeps reviews consistently useful rather than gradually abandoned.
